coredata.dc:title: Folic acid-conjugated green luminescent carbon dots as a nanoprobe for identifying folate receptor-positive cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Junli
Zhao, Xuewei
Xian, Ming
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: Early diagnosis is pivotal in subsequent prognosis and treatment of cancer. Herein, folic acid-conjugated carbon dots (FA-CDs) as a fluorescent nanoprobe were fabricated for identifying cancer cells visually. Green luminescent carbon dots (CDs) from active dry yeast (ADY) were readily prepared in scale-up to reach macroscopic production with a high yield of ~50% via a facile and rapid microwave approach. The as-prepared CDs were further combined with folic acid (FA) by covalent bonding to fabricate the FA-CDs for identification of cancer cells over-expressing folate receptor (FR). Experimental outcomes demonstrated that the resultant FA-CDs noninvasively entered into cancer cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis and could differentiate FR-positive HepG2 cells from a cell mixture by fluorescence imaging, which suggests a promising prospect of the FA-CDs as an efficient probe for cancer diagnosis and succeeding personalized therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Green luminescence
Macroscopic preparation
Fluorescent nanoprobe
Identifying cancer cells

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of the FA-CDs for targeting cancer cells
The internalization of FA-CDs into HepG2 cells via FR-mediated endocytosis
Imaging identification of the FA-CDs for cancer cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and size distribution diagrams of CDs (A) and FA-CDs (B).

UV–Vis absorption and emission spectra of CDs (λex= 360 nm) (A); PL spectra of CDs with excitation wavelengths from 300 to 500 nm with a 20 nm increment (B). Inset: photographs of CDs solution under sun light (a) and 365 nm UV light (b) and the normalized PL spectra of CDs (c).
XPS spectra of the CDs (A), and high-resolution of C1s (B), N1s (C), and O1s (D) peaks of CDs.
Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CDs.
UV–Vis absorption (A) and FTIR spectra (B) of CDs, FA and FA-CDs.

Fluorescence images of HepG2 cells after incubation for 2 h in the presence of PBS (pH 7.4) (A and E; control), FA (B and F; another control) and FA-CDs (500 μg·mL–1) (C and G); and HepG2 cells pre-treated with excess FA for FR saturation for 1 h and then incubated with FA-CDs (500 μg·mL−1) for 2 h (D and H). Dark filed (top) and merge (bottom) images. Scale bar is 20 µm.

Fluorescent images of PC12 cells (top) and HepG2 cells (bottom) after incubation for 2 h with FA-CDs (500 μg·mL−1). Ddark filed (A and D), bright filed (B and E) and merge (C and F) images. Scale bar is 20 µm.

Fluorescence images of the cell mixture of PC12 and HepG2 cells after incubation for 2 h in the absence (top) and presence (bottom; control) of FA-CDs (500 μg·mL−1). Dark filed (A and D), bright filed (B and E) and merge (C and F) images. Scale bar is 20 µm.
Preparation of the CDs and the FA-CDs.

Illustration of the internalization of FA-CDs into cancer cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis. In the absence of free FA (left), more FA-CDs enter into the cell because FA-CDs can stick to FR and thus producing bright fluorescence. In the presence of free FA (right), the competitive FA sticking to FR impedes the uptake of FA-CDs into cancer cells and hence only much feeble fluorescence.

head.ce:title: Folic acid-conjugated green luminescent carbon dots as a nanoprobe for identifying folate receptor-positive cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Center of Environmental Science and Engineering Research, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Center of Environmental Science and Engineering Research, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Washington State University, Pullman, WA 99164, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Washington State University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Early diagnosis is pivotal in subsequent prognosis and treatment of cancer. Herein, folic acid-conjugated carbon dots (FA-CDs) as a fluorescent nanoprobe were fabricated for identifying cancer cells visually. Green luminescent carbon dots (CDs) from active dry yeast (ADY) were readily prepared in scale-up to reach macroscopic production with a high yield of ~50% via a facile and rapid microwave approach. The as-prepared CDs were further combined with folic acid (FA) by covalent bonding to fabricate the FA-CDs for identification of cancer cells over-expressing folate receptor (FR). Experimental outcomes demonstrated that the resultant FA-CDs noninvasively entered into cancer cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis and could differentiate FR-positive HepG2 cells from a cell mixture by fluorescence imaging, which suggests a promising prospect of the FA-CDs as an efficient probe for cancer diagnosis and succeeding personalized therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer remains a global public health issue [1] and its early diagnosis holds great significance for premalignant changes, prognosis and therapy of carcinomas. As a normal cell progresses to a cancerous state through the complex process of transformation, cancer biomarkers play powerful roles for diagnosis of early cancer, exhibiting reliable analysis, validation, and predictive value [2]. The current methods, such as immunoassays and immunosensors [3,4], for detecting cancer biomarkers are unable to match the need in clinic. The detection for human blood or humor samples can’t afford visual and real-time monitoring of carcinoma cells. Nonetheless, advance of optical imaging in biomedicine has catalyzed cancer diagnostics research into a new dimension with the remarkable sensitivity and spatiotemporal resolution. Especially, fluorescence imaging visualizes the expressions and biochemical interactions of intracellular particular molecules in real time [5–7]. Therefore, the biomarkers are increasingly being adopted for cancer fluorescence targeting and imaging [8–10]. Folate receptor (FR) is one of important cancer biomarkers and exceptionally upregulated in many malignant epithelial cells while only minimally distributed in normal cells [11]. Different degrees of FR expression between cancer and normal cells make it possible to differentiate cancer cells by detecting FR expression. Folic acid (FA) is an ideal ligand for FR by virtue of its high specific affinity, stability and compatibility [12]. FA and its conjugates can be internalized into cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis with non-immunogenicity [13–15]. Hence the strategy that FA is linked to fluorescent nanomaterials is expected for visually probing FR thus identifying cancer cells.

Traditional quantum dots (QDs) with good optical properties have been connected with FA for labeling cancer cells [16,17]. But most QDs comprise cell-toxic heavy metal elements which suppress their biomedical utilization to some extent. So benign fluorescent nanomaterials are highly desirable toward replacing QDs. Carbon dots (CDs) [18,19], emerging as a star member in nanocarbons, have been proven to be a potent candidate of bioimaging agents [20,21]. They own alluring advantages of not only tunable emission, glorious hydrophilic and high photostability, but low cytotoxicity, admirable biocompatibility and effortless surface decoration, [22–24] superior to QDs conventionally used. Ma's group [25] first prepared FA-conjugated fluorescent CDs for distinguishing HeLa cancer cells. Liu et al. [26] reported hydrogen-bond decoration of CDs with FA toward the enhancement of optical spatial resolution of cell imaging. Lei et al. [27] further fabricated a turn-on fluorescent probe (FA-PEI-CDs) via electrostatic interaction between FA and PEI-CDs for fast and specifically permeating into HeLa and HepG2 cancer cells. Some other literatures also reported fluorescent CDs coupled with FA as recognition tags for tracing cancer cells [28–30]. Considerable efforts of these pioneers have active impacts on CDs as platform for cancer imaging diagnosis. However, the synthesis of CDs is currently restricted in improving optical properties and controlling particle size. The macroscopic preparation of CDs is of concern for the practical application purpose.

Top-down methods used in initial period, such as arc-discharge, [18] laser ablation [31] and electrochemical exfoliation, [32] are tough and high-cost for macroscopic production of CDs owing to laborious processes and harsh synthetic conditions as well as unavailable carbon precursors. However, bottom-up methods, like thermal decomposition, [33] hydrothermal treatment [34] and microwave irradiation, [35] are doable for facile, green and economical preparation due to one-pot procedure and accessible precursors. Commonly used precursors cover small organic molecules and bioresources, while the latter is more favorable in species diversity. Some research has achieved macroscopic preparation of CDs from bioresources. For example, Sahu et al. [36] made an endeavor from an easily available raw-material, orange juice, to prepare 0.4 g of CDs. Egg, [37] coffee grounds [38] and even waste foods [39] have been elected as precursors for massive synthesis of CDs as well with relatively low yields. Yu's group [40] recently attained CDs with a yield of ~38% from bee pollens via hydrothermal treatment and attempted to enlarge synthesis of CDs in proportion.

Drawing inspiration from previous studies, we tried using active dry yeast (ADY) as a precursor for preparing CDs. ADY is highly accessible food-grade yeast purchased from markets and it contains various carbonaceous compounds, such as amino acid, nucleotide, saccharide and vitamin [41]. Herein, we first used ADY as the precursor for macroscale synthesis of green-emitting CDs. The preparation was successfully scaled up with an effective yield of ~50%. To the best of our knowledge, it is the highest yield reported for preparation of green fluorescent CDs from bioresources. FA was further grafted to CDs by a classical crosslinking reaction and the resultant FA-CDs displayed great photostability, low cytocytotoxicity and brilliant biocompatibility. Competition experiment manifested that the FA-CDs could noninvasively permeate cytomembrane into cytoplasm of HepG2 cancer cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis. To put it into recognition use of cancer cells, a cell mixture of HepG2 and PC12 cells was treated with the FA-CDs. Different fluorescence phenomena from the two cells showed the FA-CDs could differentiate FR-positive HepG2 cells from the mixed cells, indicating that the FA-CDs maintained both bright green fluorescence and targeting capability.

Active dry yeast was purchased from an online flagship store of Angel Yeast Co. (Yichang, China). 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and folic acid (FA) were all purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Dialysis tubing with molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 500 Da was obtained from Union Carbide Corporation (Danbury, CT.). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) with various pH levels was prepared via standard formulations with diluted hydrochloric acid or sodium hydroxide to adjust. All experiments were carried out with ultrapure water. The ultrapure water was obtained from a Simplicity UV ultrapure water system (Merck Millipore). PC12 rat pheochromocytoma derived cell line and HepG2 human hepatoma cell line, Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM), trypsin and other related reagents were supplied by Shanxi Medical University and Gene Engineering Center of Shanxi University together.

The CDs were synthesized by carbonizing ADY via microwave radiation (Scheme 1). 0.5 g of ADY was taken with 2 mL of phosphoric acid (PA, 42–43%) into a 50 mL of glass beaker and stirred well for 10 min then radiated in a commercial microwave oven at low setting (~120 W) for 5 min. After cooling down to room temperature, a brownish black solution was acquired. The solution was neutralized with alkali and filtered through qualitative filter paper (10-15 µm) and micro-filtration membrane (0.22 µm) successively to remove large or agglomerated particles. The resulting solution was further purified via continuous dialysis using a membrane with MWCO of 500 Da for 24 h to remove excess salt and other small molecules. The purified CDs solution was yellow and transparent. The hydrosolvent was moved by freeze-drying to obtain dark-yellow CDs powder. The scale-up synthesis was executed by feeding 1, 2, 5, and 10 g of ADY under the same circumstances, respectively. Consequently, five CDs were obtained with weights of 0.257, 0.512, 0.989, 2.484, 5.270 g.

FA was conjugated to CDs by EDC/NHS reaction for fabricating FR-targeted CDs. FA (27 mg, 60 μmol) was dissolved in 5 mL of PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.4; below), then 1 mL of EDC (23 mg, 120 μmol) and 1 mL of NHS (28 mg, 240 μmol) solutions were mixed into the same reaction flask sequentially and stirred well at ambient temperature. After 30 min’ activation, 2 mL of CDs (100 mg) solution was added to the above reaction system. The solution was allowed to well stir at ambient temperature for 24 h and then continuously dialyzed to get purified FA-CDs. The FA-CDs solid powder was obtained by drying the FA-CDs solution in a lyophilizer. The overall processes were under the dark condition to protect FA from possible inactivation.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI., USA) operating at 200 kV accelerating voltage. To prepare the specimen for TEM, a drop of dialyzed CDs solution was deposited onto a copper grid with a carbon coating and then vacuum-dried at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of dried CDs powder was performed on a Krotas Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded from 4000–400 cm−1 on a Paragon1000 FTIR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) in KBr discs. UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on an U-2910 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and a F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) at room temperature. Dilute solutions in water were placed in quartz cuvettes with 1.0 cm path length. Zeta potential in PBS (0.01 M, pH 7) was examined on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 particle analyser (Malvern, UK) at 25 °C. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was determined with quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent; QY is 0.54 at 360 nm excitation) as a reference [42].

HepG2 and PC12 cell lines were cultured with a DMEM containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin-streptomycin in a humidified incubator with a 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C to adhere for 24 h. All cells used for the following were adhered state.

The cytotoxicity of FA-CDs was evaluated by flow cytometry (FCM) analysis. Briefly, adherent HepG2 cells were cultured with a series of increasing concentrations FA-CDs (0, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500 mg·mL−1) for 24 h under identical conditions. Then the cells were digested and the culture medium was abandoned. The obtained cells were washed twice with PBS and resuspended in 1 mL of PBS. The cell suspensions were transferred to measuring tubes and 5 µL of Annexin V-FITC and 5 µL of PI were added. The apoptosis analyses were performed on a flow cytometry. The cell viabilities were obtained by a simple calculation.

The cellular uptake and identification imaging of FA-CDs were examined by using HepG2 and PC12 cells on a confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). The competition experiment was conducted to confirm the internalization of FA-CDs into cancer cells via FR-mediated endocytosis. HepG2 cells were incubated with the FA-CDs (500 μg·mL−1) for 2 h. HepG2 cells were pre-treated with 100 µL of excess saturated FA solution for 1 h and then incubated with the FA-CDs for 2 h. HepG2 cells were treated purely for 2 h as blank control group and with 100 µL of saturated FA solution for 2 h as another control group. To investigate the identification capability of FA-CDs, PC12 were incubated with the FA-CDs (500 μg·mL−1) for 2 h. PC12 and HepG2 cells also were treated with the CDs (500 μg·mL−1). To further evaluate the feasibility of FA-CDs for differentiating FR-positive cancer cells, a cell mixture of HepG2 and PC12 cells were co-cultured in a near isoconcentration. The cell mixture was treated with FA-CDs (500 μg·mL−1) for 2 h. Fluorescence behaviors of each group of cells were observed under confocal microscopy. Before being observed, the cells were thoroughly washed with PBS. An excitation at 488 nm and a variable bandpass emission filter from 500 to 600 nm were chosen.

Acidic condition enables catalyze hydrolysis of proteins, main components of ADY, into amino acids. These carbonaceous substances can serve as desirable precursors for preparing CDs. The CDs were synthesized via microwaving ADY's acidic solution within minutes which benefits from microwave's rapid heating and the consequent dramatic increase in reaction rate. As shown in Scheme 1, the color of ADY's acidic solution changed from creamy-yellow to dark-brown, implying the carbonization of these carbonaceous compounds [28]. Acidic condition played dual roles of catalyzing both hydrolyzeation of protein and carbonization of precursor. The five CDs gradually increasing in weight with feeding mass increasing in proportion were produced with the yield of 50.9% ± 1.3%. What’ more, the mean yield in terms of synthesis of CDs from 0.5 g of mass for five times was 50.36% ± 0.86%. Though more repeated experiments entail to be enforced, such small standard deviations suggest that our method is repeatable and reproducible. Compared with those reported bioresource-derived CDs (Table S1), obviously, the green-emitting CDs in this work is superior to blue-emitting CDs at optimal excitation. For bioimaging, blue fluorescence may cause interference of biological background [26]. Most importantly, the yield of ~50% in this work is much higher than other reported yields. It is the currently highest record, as fa as we know, for green luminescent CDs preparation from bioresources. Macroscopic synthesis with such high yield has great significance for actual application of CDs.

The morphology and size distribution of CDs were analyzed as shown in Fig. 1A. The TEM analysis indicated that the CDs were spherical and monodispersed and exhibited a size distribution of 2–6 nm with an average diameter about 3.4 nm. The UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs were obtained as depicted in Fig. 2. From the UV–Vis spectrum, the strong absorption at 280 nm was observed, which was ascribed to n~π* electron transition of the C=O band [27]. The photographs of CDs dilute solution showed light yellow under natural light and green under 365 nm UV lamp (Inset Fig. 2a and b), revealing that the CDs exhibited green fluorescence. The PL spectra of CDs showed a strong emission peak at a center of 516 nm under different excitation wavelengths. To further confirm the clear excitation-independent behavior, the PL spectra of CDs were normalized. As shown in Inset Fig. 2c, with increasing excitation wavelength, the emission peaks almost no shifted to longer wavelength. This behavior should be the result of the surface molecular state and particle size distribution of the CDs [43]. It potentially implied the presence of abundant groups and self-passivation of surface traps of CDs [44]. The CDs solution remained clear and transparent under ambient temperature for two months, revealing their excellent solubility in water. The QY determined of CDs was 11.6% (using quinine sulfate as a reference). The PL stability of CDs was examined in different conditions. The luminous CDs had non-quenching and excellent photostability (Fig. S1).

XPS and FTIR analyses were carried out to investigate the structure and composition of CDs. As can be seen in Fig. 3A, the raw XPS data indicated that the CDs primarily consisted of three elements: carbon (48%), nitrogen (11%), and oxygen (33%). The C1s spectrum in Fig. 3B showed four different bonding energy peaks at 284.8, 285.4, 286.2, and 287.9 eV, corresponding to C=C, C–N, C–OH, C=O/O–C=O groups, respectively [36]. The N1s spectrum in Fig. 3C exhibited three peaks at 399.5, 400.0 and 401.4 eV, assigning to the presence of N–C, N–O and N–H (primary/secondary amine) structures, respectively [44]. With regard to the O1s spectrum (Fig. 3D), three peaks at 531.5, 532.2 and 532.8 eV were attributed to C=O, O=C–OH and O–H groups, respectively [45]. FTIR spectrum of the CDs similarly showed the presence of –OH, –COOH and –NH2 groups on their surface. As shown in Fig. 4, the broad absorption peak around 3350 cm ascribed to the OH or/and NH stretching vibration, the peak at 1541 cm coincided with the NH in-plane bending vibration of NH2, and the peaks at 1650, 1401, 1251 and 1051 cm agreed with the C=O, C=C, C–O and C–N stretching vibrations, respectively [40,45]. The rich surface groups enabled the CDs be decorated with functional molecules expediently.

FA is readily combined to other molecules through gamma-carboxyl group, resulting in no reduction of the affinity. The amino groups of CDs make them to be coupled with the carboxyl groups of FA conveniently. In this work, the CDs were covalently conjugated to FA through EDC/NHS reaction. After the reaction completion, the excess FA was removed by continuous dialysis until no change of UV–vis absorption of solution in dialysis tube occurred. As shown in Fig. 5A, the characteristic peaks at 283 nm and 345 nm representing FA were obviously observed in the UV–vis absorption of FA-CDs, indicating that the successful immobilization of FA onto the surface of CDs [25]. The amount of FA immobilized onto the CDs was approximately 309 μg·mg−1 by measuring the absorption at 283 nm. Moreover, zeta-potential measurements, FTIR spectra and TEM images also evidenced the formation of FA-CDs. Zeta potential of FA-CDs, CDs and FA were recorded at pH 7. The zeta-potential of CDs decreased from −6.7 mV to −16.2 mV after reacted with FA, because the amino groups of CDs were covered by FA with a negative charge of −31.6 mV [44]. FTIR spectra of FA-CDs, CDs and FA were obtained as shown in Fig. 5B. The infrared absorption of FA-CDs exhibited characteristic peaks at 1482, 1606 and 1695 cm−1 (phenyl and pterin rings) which were indicative of FA [25,46]. Besides, from the Fig. 1, the particle sizes of the FA-CDs (size distribution: 2–6 nm; average diameter: 3.8 nm) were a bit bigger than that of CDs (size distribution: 3–8 nm; average diameter: 4.1 nm). The sizes of the CDs increased after conjugation with FA, revealing that FA was favourably conjugated to the CDs [47,48]. In addition, the monodispersed FA-CDs showed that aggregation of particles didn’t take place.

The effect of modification of FA on the PL stability of FA-CDs was evaluated (Fig. S2). After FA-modification, only slight variation appeared in fluorescence spectra of the FA-CDs and the CDs. The influences of pH, ionic strength, storage time and UV exposure on the fluorescent stability of FA-CDs were studied (Fig. S1). The FA-CDs held almost constant fluorescence strength at pH between 3 and 11. No obvious intensity change was observed in a series of CDs solutions containing increasing concentration of NaCl. Photobleaching didn’t occur when the CDs solution was placed under UV light (365 nm) for 6 h. Optical intensity of CDs also didn’t generate distinct decrease after 10 days storage at ambient temperature in sun light. These findings demonstrated that the FA-CDs had admirable photostability. Importantly, the effects of common cellular species on the fluorescence of FA-CDs were examined (Fig. S3). No considerable interference was observed, suggesting that the FA-CDs were suitable for cellular environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the FA-CDs for targeting cancer cells

ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatibility has considerable impact on nanomaterials for cell imaging. To evaluate the toxicity of FA-CDs to live cells, the concentration-dependent effect of FA-CDs on cell viability was tested by flow cytometry analysis. As shown in Fig. S4, after cultivation with increasing concentration of the FA-CDs for 24 h, the viability of HepG2 cells exhibited no significant decrease and even remained above 85% under a substantial concentration of 500 mg·mL−1, which illuminated that the FA-CDs had low cytotoxicity and super biocompatibility. During the next imaging experiment, cellular morphological damage sign was scarcely observed from fluorescence images, further emphasizing the non-invasiveness of FA-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The internalization of FA-CDs into HepG2 cells via FR-mediated endocytosis
ce:section.ce:para: The internalization of FA-CDs into HepG2 cancer cells was investigated. The used FR-positive HepG2 cells [27] were supposed to endocytose the FA-CDs via FR-mediated delivery. The excitation wavelength of 488 nm was chosen to avoid the possible disturbing influence of background fluorescence from the cells and FA. As shown in Fig. 6A and B, no auto-fluorescence was detected. However, bright green fluorescence was observed in membrane and cytoplasm but nucleus of the cells incubated with FA-CDs for 2 h (Fig. 6C and G), indicating that the FA-CDs could stay in membrane and permeate into cytoplasm rather than nucleus. This is consistent with the fact that FR mainly exists in the cancer cell membrane and can effectively deliver the coupled-FA substances into cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis. When HepG2 cells were beforehand saturated with excess free FA for 1 h and following incubated with FA-CDs for 2 h under the same conditions, because the free FA competitively occupied FR to prevent FA-CDs from entering into HepG2 cells, mostly no fluorescence appeared as showed in Fig. 6D and H, in keeping with the reported article, [25] which further verified that the FR-mediated endocytosis delivered the FA-CDs conjugates into cancer cells. The mechanism of internalization of FA-CDs into HepG2 cells is described in Scheme 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging identification of the FA-CDs for cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: The identification ability of FA-CDs for cancer cells was estimated with PC12 and HepG2 cells. PC12 cells are different from HepG2 cells in the level of FR expression [49]. The FA-CDs should adhere to FR-positive HepG2 cells spontaneously due to the high affinity binding of FA to FR but FR-negative PC12 cells. As anticipated, after 2 h incubation with the FA-CDs, HepG2 cells ( Fig. 7D) exhibited much stronger green fluorescence than PC12 cells (Fig. 7A). The two cells from blank controls displayed almost no fluorescence (Fig. S5). But the other two controls where PC12 and HepG2 cells were incubated with CDs both exhibited feeble fluorescence (Fig. S6). The result indicated that the conjugation of FA endowed the CDs with ability for targeting FR-positive cancer cells.

A co-culture mixture of HepG2 and PC12 cells was subjected to further evaluate the feasibility of FA-CDs for differentiating FR-positive cancer cells. Besides the disparate level of FR expression, another considered reason selecting HepG2 and PC12 cells was that fusiform HepG2 cells had a cytomorphological difference from that of round PC12 cells, [49] which facilitated to observe targeting phenomenon. As shown in Fig. 8, in the cell mixture model, HepG2 cells showed bright fluorescence after 2 h incubation with the FA-CDs whereas PC12 cells did not. The completely different fluorescence phenomena demonstrated the FA-CDs could differentiate FR-positive HepG2 cells from the cell mixture, which manifested that the FA-CDs are applicable as a potential tool for identifying FR-positive cancer cells.

In summary, an efficient nanoprobe based-on the FA-CDs was used for targeting and imaging FR-positive cancer cells successfully. The macroscopic production of bright green fluorescent CDs from a new bioresource, active dry yeast, was implemented by a one-pot, high-yield and scale-up synthetic approach with good repeatability and reproducibility, which is of significance for the practical use of CDs. After conjugation of FA to the CDs, the photostable, low-cytotoxic and biocompatible FA-CDs could enter into HepG2 cancer cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis, which was certified by the competition experiment. And the applicability of FA-CDs to distinguish FR-positive cancer cells was demonstrated by analyzing the cell mixture of PC12 and HepG2 cells. The FA-CDs have great prospects for cancer studies in early diagnosis, prognosis and treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21475080 and 21575084), the Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (No. 2017-K1) and the Hundred Talent Programme of Shanxi Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.02.009.
